feedback.text: Please let me respond on this call for evidence from the perspective of CoolGames, an independent Dutch game studio. And I also want to refer to the excellent call-out of Supercells CEO Ilkka Paananen and many leading European gaming leaders (Lets not kill one of Europes few tech success stories): https://www.linkedin.com/posts/ilkkapaananen_lets-not-kill-one-of-europes-few-tech-success-activity-7381554010337554432-db_j?utm_source=share&utm_medium=member_desktop&rcm=ACoAAACpm3UBJeTW3XQ_fyFFRBM7-C8mc2Iosgg CoolGames is a Dutch developer and publisher of casual videogames with millions of players worldwide. Our growing studio, based in Amsterdam, currently employs nearly 70 people who are working on both existing and new game productions. In recent years, we have found good success with several popular in-house developed titles, many of which are based on the so-called free-to-play model: players can play the game completely for free, while occasionally seeing advertisements. In addition, they have the option to make payments within the game for example, if they want something extra that the free players dont immediately have access to, and often with the use of virtual currencies. This is the leading model for almost all game publishers, both in the app-stores and among game distributors via websites and the gaming platforms of major tech companies a segment in which CoolGames is particularly active. Our business model for offering games for free is therefore based on a combination of advertising revenue and optional microtransactions. However, in reality, about 99% of our players never do any in-game purchase and therefore continue to play the game entirely for free. But the very small group of players who do want to enjoy an extra experience in the game and are willing to voluntarily pay for it are very important to our overall revenue mix. As a Netherlands-based studio with very labour-intensive game projects, we cannot be 100% dependent on an advertising model alone. Just like most other costly media productions, such as feature films, streaming TV services or quality journalism, voluntary in-game payments are an indispensable part of the business model, allowing us continued investments in new productions. And we believe that the free-to-play model actually benefits the players strongly: the income from voluntary purchases in the game, prevents an excessive advertising burden on consumers and enables us to make new investments, continue innovating, and develop new European games IP. European game developers like CoolGames who already have to compete against much larger American and Asian game studios and tech platforms would suffer significant harm if voluntary in-game currencies were to be banned. Of course we fully say NO to any misleading or aggressive payment practices. And purchases generally take place at the platform level of those who distribute our games, where protection measures are in place such as passwords, PINs, parental control tools, and verified parental consent. And should there, despite such measures still be complaints about unjustified payments, its our policy to refund those immediately. But together with many other European game leaders like Ilkka, and for the tens of thousands of professionals working in the European games industry, we would like to ask to take this into account, before imposing sweeping measures that most likely will not improve the player experience. Moreover, they potentially endanger the future of 6000 European game studios, especially in one of the very few global tech-markets where Europe actually managed to play a significant role, amongst the competition of American and Asian giants. Laurens Rutten, Founder and CEO of CoolGames
